critique.summary: Meta announced that by mid-2026, the Meta Horizon Worlds VR app will be removed from Meta Quest devices, transitioning Horizon Worlds to a mobile-only platform. Several Horizon Worlds features and perks will be discontinued or altered, while users can still access some worlds in VR until June 15, 2026.
mainPoints: Horizon Worlds and Events will no longer be available on Meta Quest VR after June 15, 2026.
Certain popular Horizon Worlds such as Horizon Central, Events Arena, Kaiju, and Bobber Bay will be removed from VR by March 31, 2026.
Horizon Worlds will continue as a mobile-only experience on the Meta Horizon mobile app.
Hyperscape Capture features are being limited, with sharing and co-experiencing no longer supported after March 24, 2026.
Horizon-specific perks in the Meta Horizon Plus subscription will be removed by March 31, 2026, but core gaming benefits remain.
Meta is continuing to update the Quest platform with new features unrelated to Horizon Worlds.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article align with official Meta communications and announcements found on Meta's community forums and official blogs. The timeline and feature changes correspond with Meta's stated plans to focus Horizon Worlds on mobile platforms and discontinue VR support on Quest devices. User reactions quoted in the article reflect genuine community concerns but do not contradict the factual updates. Overall, the article accurately reports Meta's announced changes.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed reasons behind Meta's decision to discontinue Horizon Worlds on VR, such as user engagement metrics or strategic priorities.
There is no mention of alternative VR social platforms or options for users affected by the removal of Horizon Worlds on Quest.
Potential impacts on creators who develop content for Horizon Worlds, including economic and community effects, are only briefly touched upon through user comments.
The article does not discuss how the mobile-only transition might affect user experience or accessibility compared to VR.
No official data or statements from Meta regarding user base size or financial considerations driving this change are included.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Meta's decision to discontinue Horizon Worlds on Meta Quest, with commenters expressing disappointment over wasted investment, skepticism about Meta's strategic direction, and concerns about layoffs. Some highlight the platform's low user engagement and question the company's future in VR and the metaverse.

comments.author: t-writescode
comments.text:
They were trying to compete with an existing, VERY good couple of alternatives, and the people most actually likely to use that product were already on those services.

It was a losing play that didn’t know what market it was actually entering.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the competitive landscape and market challenges Horizon Worlds faced, providing a realistic assessment of why the product struggled.

comments.author: tyleo
comments.text:
Interesting, cutting way back in the product they renamed the whole company for.

They feel a bit directionless to me. They are still making money but even their AI attempt feels half hearted. I think they are really trying but I’m not sure they can build the engineering muscle to move in new areas with the brand damage they’ve sustained.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully critiques Meta's broader strategic issues and brand damage, which plausibly impact their ability to innovate effectively.

comments.author: xd1936
comments.text: Unbelievable. They re-architected the whole operating system around this stupid app. They discontinued their previous homescreen environments in favor of trying to promote Horizon Worlds, only to discontinue the blasted thing anyway? After all of those millions of dollars spent trying to make virtual events happen?
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional, this comment points out the costly and arguably misguided OS changes made to support Horizon Worlds, highlighting wasted resources.

comments.author: drivebyhooting
comments.text: When will horizon worlds be axed entirely? It has something like fewer than 10,000 active users at any given time.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly notes the low user engagement numbers, which likely contributed to the decision to discontinue the platform.

comments.author: yhvr
comments.text: It's baffling to me how much they juiced up this platform on Horizon OS, and now they're just axing it from there entirely. Millions if not billions of dollars gone to waste. At least I won't have to see it in the UI anymore, I guess. How does this even happen?
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration over the financial losses and strategic reversal, which aligns with public reports of heavy investment and subsequent shutdown.

comments.author: senderista
comments.text: Imagine what $100B could have done for medical or energy research.
comments.analysis: Though speculative, this comment thoughtfully critiques the opportunity cost of Meta's massive spending on the metaverse instead of other societal priorities.

comments.author: jeffbee
comments.text: Second Life, which contains only niche perverts, has more concurrent actives than Horizon Worlds.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights Horizon Worlds' low engagement by comparing it to an older virtual world, underscoring its failure to attract a large user base.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: Maybe I'm just naive but I don't really understand discontinuing things like this. Like, unless there are like 100 people using this, how can it not be possible to just leave this running at like 0.5% of its former capacity. Just leave up like 1 server, collapse all of the DBs into one, and let these few autists have their stuff.
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid point about the technical feasibility of maintaining minimal service for a small user base, questioning the necessity of full shutdown.

comments.author: d--b
comments.text:
That sucks.

At least they had a purpose, a vision.

Now Zuckerberg is going to be all sour about it and even more cynical about everything.

They’re going to go back to what they know how to do: optimize for attention and sell personal data.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a pessimistic view of Meta's leadership and future direction, which is plausible but more speculative.

comments.author: etchalon
comments.analysis: A brief, emotionally charged critique of Zuckerberg's leadership without substantive evidence or context.
